revision.text:
[[cs:Heidžó-kjó]][[de:Heijō-kyō]][[en:Heijō-kyō]][[es:Heijō-kyō]][[eu:Heijo-kyo]][[fa:هیجو-کیو]][[fi:Heijō-kyō]][[fr:Heijō-kyō]][[gor:Heijō-kyō]][[hu:Heidzsó-kjó]][[id:Heijō-kyō]][[it:Heijō-kyō]][[ja:平城京]][[ko:헤이조쿄]][[la:Heijō-kyō]][[min:Heijō-kyō]][[ms:Heijō-kyō]][[pl:Heijō-kyō]][[pt:Heijō-kyō]][[ru:Хэйдзё-кё]][[sh:Heijō-kyō]][[simple:Heijō-kyō]][[sl:Heidžo-kjo]][[th:เฮโจเกียว]][[tr:Heijō-kyō]][[uk:Хейдзьо-кьо]][[vi:Heijō-kyō]][[wuu:平城京]][[zh-classical:平城京]][[zh-yue:平城京]][[zh:平城京]] {{Short description|Historical capital of Japan}} {{distinguish|Heizei|Saga|Junna}} {{No footnotes|date=May 2012}} [[File:140531 Heijo-kyo Nara Japan01bs.jpg|thumb|300px|Heijō-kyō ruins]] [[File:220325 One-thousandth scale model of Heijo Kyo.jpg|thumb|350px|1/1000 scale model of Heijō-kyō, held by Nara City Hall]] [[File:Heijokyo.jpg|thumb|Groundplan of Heijō-kyō]] [[File:Heijokyo - Map of major buildings.png|thumb|Closer map of the major sites of Heijō-kyō]] [[File:Sakitatanamikofungun.jpg|thumb|Satellite view of Heijō-kyō site ({{lang|ja|平城宮趾}}) ]] [[File:Miniature Model of Heijo Palace Daigokuden.jpg|thumb|Miniature model of the [[Heijō Palace]]]] [[File:181103 Heijo Palace Daigokuden Nara Japan05n.jpg|thumb|The ''[[Daigokuden]]'', the main building to Heijo Palace]] [[File:Heijokyo Suzakumon 05.JPG|thumb|The ''{{ill|Suzakumon|en|Suzakumon|qid=Q211614|lt=Suzakumon|1=Suzakumon}}'', the main entrance to Heijo Palace]] {{Nihongo|'''Heijō-kyō'''|平城京|4=also ''Heizei-kyō'', sometimes ''Nara no miyako''}} was the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|qid=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan|1=Capital of Japan}} during most of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, from 710 to 740 and again from 745 to 784. The [[Heijō Palace|imperial palace]] is a listed {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Heritage|1=World Heritage Site}} together with other places in the city of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}} (cf. {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|qid=Q1055749|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara}}). {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|qid=Q233224|lt=Empress Genmei|1=Empress Genmei}} ordered the Imperial capital moved from Fujiwara-kyō to Heijō-kyō in 708, and the move to Heijō-kyō was complete in 710. Heijō-kyō was modeled after {{ill|Chang'an|en|Chang'an|qid=Q6501000|lt=Chang'an|1=Chang'an}}, the capital of {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang-dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, although Heijō-kyō lacked walls. In the city, merchants and traders from China, Korea and India introduced various foreign cultures to Heijō-kyō through the Silk Road. As a result, Heijō-kyō flourished as Japan's first international and political capital, with a peak population of between 50,000 and 100,000.{{cite web|url=https://www.nabunken.go.jp/nabunkenblog/2014/09/tanken48.html|script-title=ja:（４８）平城京の人口と役人たち|website=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|access-date=August 16, 2022}} The overall form of the city was an irregular rectangle, and the area of the city was more than 25 km2. ==Architecture== In the area of Heijō-kyō, there are ancient Buddhist temples, and some temples are also listed as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Heritage|1=World Heritage Site}} together with [[Heijō Palace]]. * {{ill|Daian-ji|en|Daian-ji|qid=Q1044009|lt=Daian-ji|1=Daian-ji}} (大安寺) * Daikokuden (大極殿, reconstruction) * {{ill|Gangō-ji|en|Gangō-ji|qid=Q1203099|lt=Gangō-ji|1=Gangō-ji}} (元興寺) * [[Kōfuku-ji]] (興福寺) * [[Saidai-ji]] (西大寺) * {{ill|Suzakumon|en|Suzakumon|qid=Q211614|lt=Suzakumon|1=Suzakumon}} (朱雀門, reconstruction) * [[Tōdai-ji]] (東大寺) * [[Yakushi-ji]] (薬師寺) ==1,300th anniversary== The year 2010 marked the passage of 1,300 years since the establishment of Nara Heijō-kyō. Commemorative events of the 1,300th anniversary of Nara Heijō-kyō Capital (Japanese:平城遷都1300th year 祭) were held in and around Nara Prefecture from April 24 to November 7, 2010. These events included special displays of {{ill|national treasure|en|national treasure|qid=Q843286|lt=national treasure|1=National treasure}}s and other cultural properties, walking events that explore famous places in Nara and traditional events in various places throughout Nara. * Main Event Site – [[Heijō Palace|Heijō-kyō Capital Area]] (平城宮跡) 　　　A：Entrance Plaza
　　　　　●Heijō Palace Site Tour Center
　　　　　●Corporate Participation Hall
　　　B：Heijō History Museum/Full-Scale Replica of Japanese Diplomatic Ship for {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|en|Japanese missions to Tang China|qid=Q262529|lt=Envoys to Tang China|1=Japanese missions to Tang China}}
　　　C：Suzaku Gate Plaza
　　　　　●Suzaku Gate
　　　D：Exchange Plaza
　　　　　●Mahoroba Stage
　　　　　●Exchange Hall
　　　E：Heijō Palace Site Museum
　　　F：Front Courtyard of the Former Imperial Audience Hall
　　　G：South Gate Plaza
　　　　　●Tempyo period costume rental area
　　　H：Heijō-kyō Hands-on Learning Plaza
　　　　　●Heijō-kyō Hands-on Learning Center
　　　　　●Ministry of the Imperial Household
　　　I：Excavation Site Exhibition Hall
　　　J：Eastern Palace Garden Plaza
　　　　　●Eastern Palace Garden * Other Events Site 　　　Ikaruga and Shigi-san Areas (cf. {{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|en|Ikaruga, Nara|qid=Q379233|lt=Ikaruga|1=Ikaruga, Nara}}, 斑鳩・信貴山)
　　　Asuka and Fujiwara Areas (cf. [[Asuka, Yamato|Asuka]], 飛鳥・藤原)
　　　Katsuragi Area (葛城)
　　　Yoshino Area (cf. {{ill|Mount Yoshino|en|Mount Yoshino|qid=Q1036938|lt=Mount Yoshino|1=Mount Yoshino}}, 吉野)
　　　Yamato Kogen Plains and Uda Area (大和高原・宇陀) ==See also== *[[Heijō Palace]] – Imperial palace *{{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|qid=Q1055749|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara}} – UNESCO World Heritage Site *{{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|qid=Q20982902|lt=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} ==References== {{Reflist}} *{{cite book | last = Martin | first = John H. |author2=Phyllis G. Martin | title = Nara: A Cultural Guide to Japan's Ancient Capital | publisher = Tuttle Publishing | year = 1993 | pages =11–14 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=D4ZR39KI0PkC | isbn = 0-8048-1914-9 }} * Yoko Hsueh Shirai. ''Envisioning Heijokyo: 100 Questions & Answers about the Ancient Capital in Nara''. CreateSpace Independent Publishing Platform. 2011. {{ISBN|978-1463768225}} ==External links== {{commons Category-inline}} * [http://www.nabunken.go.jp/heijo/museum/index.html Nara Palace Site Museum]{{in lang|ja}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100413114017/http://www.1300.jp/foreign/english/index.html The Commemorative Events for the 1300th Anniversary] (Available in Chinese, English, French, Korean and Japanese) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20220707112029/http://www.hgeo.h.kyoto-u.ac.jp/soramitsu/heijokyo.html Jô-Bô System of Heijô-Kyô] * [http://www.hgeo.h.kyoto-u.ac.jp/soramitsu/Nara.html Nara (Heijô-kyô) — The Capital of Japan in the 8th Century] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130704145857/http://www.hgeo.h.kyoto-u.ac.jp/soramitsu/Nara.html |date=2013-07-04 }} * [https://archive.today/20130218203610/http://www.takenaka.co.jp/takenaka_e/majorworks_e/topics/2010/sum/03.html Takenaka Corporation on the reconstruction of the first Daigokuden Palace rebuilt at the Special Historical Site-Designated Heijokyu Ruins] {{s-start}} {{succession box|title=Capital of Japan| before={{ill|Fujiwara-kyō|en|Fujiwara-kyō|qid=Q1196381|lt=Fujiwara-kyō|1=Fujiwara-kyō}}|after={{ill|Kuni-kyō|en|Kuni-kyō|qid=Q1155335|lt=Kuni-kyō|1=Kuni-kyō}}|years=710–740}} {{succession box|title=Capital of Japan| before={{ill|Shigaraki Palace|en|Shigaraki Palace|qid=Q262438|lt=Shigaraki Palace|1=Shigaraki Palace}}|after=[[Nagaoka-kyō]]|years=745–784}} {{s-end}}
